title: Chapter 9: Business War
content: Liang Yuan made his move on a Tuesday.

The cousin—forty-one, silver-templed, perpetually scheming—had been circling Xue's division of Liang Corp for months. He controlled the family's western operations: shipping, logistics, and a network of "consulting firms" that provided services no one asked too many questions about. His ambition was the worst kind: patient, well-funded, and willing to burn the house down to become the one standing in the ashes.
The takeover was elegant. Ren had to give him that.

Yuan had spent six months quietly acquiring Liang Corp stock through shell companies—never more than 2% per entity, always below disclosure thresholds. Then, three days ago, he'd triggered a cascade: a coordinated sell-off by five institutional investors who'd been "advised" that Liang Corp's eastern division was under investigation for customs fraud. The same fraud the Zhao family had tried to pin on Xue's operation.
Only this time, the evidence wasn't forged. This time, someone inside Xue's own team had fed real documents to real investigators.
The mole.

Ren had been tracking the Zhao family's kitchen spy—the voice he'd heard during the assassination aftermath—but this was different. This was a Liang family insider, working for Yuan, using the Zhao operation as cover.
*Two moles. Two different employers. The same target: Liang Xue.*

The board meeting was scheduled for Friday. If Yuan could convince a majority of directors that Xue's division was a liability, he'd force a restructuring that stripped her of operational control. She'd become a figurehead—bloodline valuable, strategically neutered.
And a neutered Liang Xue meant less protection for Ren, less access to the family's intelligence network, and a shorter leash on the causal backdoor that kept Earth intact.
Ren couldn't let that happen.

He sat in the library, the Salvation System running counter-intelligence analysis on the data streams now accessible through his eastern intelligence credentials. The mole's digital fingerprint was there—subtle, professional, but not invisible. A series of document accesses that didn't match the user's normal pattern. A login from a VPN endpoint in the western district during hours when the user claimed to be at home. An email to a burner account that contained a single word: *greenlight*.

The mole was Vice Director Chen—the head of Xue's customs compliance team. A twenty-year company veteran with a spotless record and a gambling problem that Yuan had been bankrolling for eighteen months.
Standard leverage recruitment. The kind of thing that worked best on people who were good at their jobs but terrible at their lives.
Ren had the evidence. The question was how to use it.

He couldn't bring it to Xue directly—not again. She'd accepted his help once, but that had been external intelligence about the Zhao family. This was *internal*. A mole in her own division meant she'd been compromised on her own watch. Admitting that an outsider had found what she'd missed would damage her authority at precisely the moment she couldn't afford weakness.
*She needs to discover this herself. But she needs to know where to look.*

The War God's strategic mind worked through the problem like a lock-picker feeling tumblers fall. Xue was smart. If he could point her toward the right questions, she'd find the answers on her own. The key was planting the seed without being seen planting it.
That evening, Ren intercepted Sun Wei in the service corridor—the assistant who'd become his unofficial pipeline to Xue.
"The customs fraud investigation—has anyone checked whether the compromised documents were accessed from inside the division?"
Sun Wei blinked. "What do you mean?"
"I mean the Zhao family used forged documents last time. This time, the documents are real. Someone had to leak them. The question is whether the leak came from outside or inside."
Sun Wei's face went pale. The implications were landing.
"Who has access to customs compliance files?"
"Vice Director Chen. His team. And Mrs. Liang." A pause. "And the board's audit committee, but they're all family members or long-term appointees."
"Check Chen's access logs. Look for anomalies—logins from unusual locations, off-hours document views, emails to external accounts."
"Mr. Zhou, I can't just—"

"You can. Because if you're the one who finds the mole, Mrs. Liang will remember that you were loyal when others weren't. And loyalty in this family—" Ren held the assistant's gaze. "—is worth more than gold."
Sun Wei swallowed hard. Then he nodded and disappeared down the corridor.
Twenty-four hours later, the mole was exposed.

Liang Xue presented the evidence at an emergency board meeting with the clinical precision of a surgeon removing a tumor. Chen was terminated. The western investors were contacted with proof that the "investigation" was fabricated and the sell-off was orchestrated. Yuan's shell companies were identified and reported to the securities commission.
Xue's division was not only preserved—it was strengthened. The board voted to expand her operational authority, citing her "proactive security measures" and "decisive crisis management."

Liang Yuan was invited to a private conversation with the Patriarch. No one knew what was said, but Yuan emerged white-faced and visibly shaken, and his consulting firms quietly dissolved over the following week.

Ren watched the aftermath from the library, the Salvation System cataloging the shifting power dynamics within the family. Xue's position was stronger. Yuan's was diminished. The Patriarch had used the crisis to remind everyone who was really in charge.
And Ren had maneuvered the entire thing without his name appearing in a single report.
*Invisible influence. The most powerful kind.*
He was reaching for a book when the library door opened.

Liang Xue walked in—not in formal business attire, but in a simple cashmere sweater and trousers, her hair loose. She looked tired. The kind of tired that came from fighting a war on three fronts: corporate, familial, and whatever wars she was waging that he hadn't discovered yet.
"You." She stopped five feet away, arms crossed. "Sun Wei came to me with the idea of checking access logs. Then he admitted you'd suggested it."
Ren said nothing.
"How did you know about Chen?"
"His gambling debts are a matter of public record if you know where to look. The casino connection traces back to one of Yuan's holding companies. It wasn't difficult."
"It was *impossible*. I had three security teams reviewing that data for weeks and none of them found it."
"Then your security teams aren't looking at the right things."
The words hung between them. Xue's eyes searched his face—not for deception, Ren realized, but for understanding. She was trying to reconcile the man she'd married with the man who kept saving her.
*Don't look too close,* he thought. *You won't like what you find.*
"Every time I think I understand you," Xue said quietly, "you do something that makes me realize I don't understand you at all."
"Maybe that's a good thing."

"Maybe." She uncrossed her arms—a subtle release of tension. "The board expanded my authority. I'm now overseeing all eastern and central operations. That means more travel, more meetings, more exposure."
"More targets on your back."
"Yes." She met his eyes directly. "I need someone I can trust to monitor things when I'm not here. Someone who sees what my security teams miss."
"Are you asking me to be your inside man?"
"I'm asking you to be my *partner*."
The word landed like a bomb. Partner. Not husband-in-name-only. Not useless son-in-law. *Partner.* With everything that implied—trust, shared purpose, mutual obligation.
The Salvation System:

`CAUSAL BACKDOOR: Fundamentally reinforced`
`SUBJECT TRUST METRIC: 27% → 41%`
`RELATIONSHIP STATUS: Transactional → Strategic alliance`
`NOTE: This is not romance. This is survival. But survival partnerships have been the foundation of greater things.`
Forty-one percent. Still below half. But the architecture of their relationship had shifted from a cage to a coalition.
"I'll do it," Ren said. "On one condition."
"Name it."
"Tell me about the old wing. The restricted area. I know it's important—I don't need to go inside. But I need to understand what I'm protecting when I'm monitoring things in your absence."
Xue's expression shuttered instantly. The warmth that had crept into her voice froze over. "That's not something I can discuss."
"Xue—"

"The restricted wing is family business, Ren. Not partner business. Not yet." Her jaw was tight, her hand unconsciously touching the jade pendant. "Ask me again in six months. Maybe I'll have an answer."
She left without another word.
But the jade pendant had pulsed when she touched it—a brief, bright flare of array energy that the Salvation System captured and filed.

`PENDANT REACTION: Emotional resonance triggered`
`INTERPRETATION: Liang Xue knows more about the restricted wing than she's saying. And the pendant records her emotional state when she thinks about it.`
`DATA POINT: Fear. The pendant detected fear.`
Xue was afraid of what was in the restricted wing.
Which meant she wasn't wholly ignorant of her family's darker projects. She knew *something*. And she was choosing not to look too closely—a survival strategy that Ren recognized all too well.
*She's protecting herself by not knowing.* The same way a soldier learned not to read casualty reports. The less you knew, the less they could make you complicit in.
But ignorance wasn't a luxury they could afford. Not with 1086 days on the clock.

Ren opened his laptop and began building an intelligence file on Vice Director Chen—not the man's work history, but his personal network. Who recruited him. Who else Yuan had leverage over. What other moles might be waiting in the walls.

The game was layers now. Surface: corporate politics. Middle: family power dynamics. Deep: the dimensional array and the Abyss waiting beyond. And somewhere in between, a woman who was afraid of her own family's secrets and a man who'd come back from the dead to make sure those secrets didn't kill everyone on the planet.
Step by step. Day by day. One crisis at a time.
His phone buzzed. The encrypted channel.
*"The Patriarch found Node 5. A bronze bell hidden in a temple in the western mountains. It's being transported to the estate. You have a 72-hour window before it's integrated into the array."*
Liu Haoran. The voice from the future.
*"72 hours, Zhou. What are you going to do?"*
Ren looked at the message for a long time. Then he typed:
*"I'm going to find a way to be in the room when it arrives."*
He closed the phone and started planning.
One thousand eighty-six days.
